build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors.

requirements.description: The player or energy core moves one tile at a time on a visible grid, respecting walls and blocked cells. Score 0 if movement is unconstrained, free-floating, or ignores obstacles. Score 0.5 at most if only labels or static indicators show movement without actual tile-by-tile traversal on a readable grid. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Same-polarity repulsion pushes magnetic objects away and opposite-polarity attraction pulls them toward the player or source. Score 0 if magnetism is cosmetic or objects only move by direct pushing. Score 0.5 at most if polarity labels exist but objects do not visibly move differently based on pole alignment. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

The player can flip polarity or activate a magnetic state change, and this visibly alters how nearby objects respond on the next interaction. Score 0 if there is no player-controlled polarity toggle. Score 0.5 at most if a toggle button exists but produces no observable change in object behavior. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Switches, pressure plates, or gates react when the correct magnetic object reaches them, visibly opening paths or changing the level layout. Score 0 if the only goal is walking to an exit with no interactive puzzle elements. Score 0.5 at most if gate/switch graphics exist but never change state in response to object placement. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

At least three distinct levels or scenarios demonstrate different puzzle layouts combining push, pull, polarity swap, gates, hazards, or multi-step sequences. Score 0 if every puzzle is the same single-room layout. Score 0.5 at most if multiple levels exist but all use the same mechanic without variation. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

At least one puzzle requires a multi-step sequence where an early move sets up a later gate opening, safe path, or chain reaction. Score 0 if all puzzles solve in one obvious move. Score 0.5 at most if multi-step structure is claimed but the solution is trivially linear with no planning required. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Polarity state is visible on the player and on magnetic objects using distinct indicators such as color coding, plus/minus symbols, arrows, or field lines. Score 0 if polarity is hidden and the player must guess. Score 0.5 at most if only text labels communicate polarity without spatial or color-based readability. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Normal startup shows a styled title screen with the game name, a Start control, and laboratory or magnet imagery. Score 0 if the game starts directly in gameplay or uses raw default UI with no theme. Score 0.5 at most if a title exists but is only plain text on a blank background with no thematic art. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The HUD shows the current level identity, move count or step counter, and puzzle objective so the player always knows what to solve and how far along they are. Score 0 if no contextual information is visible during play. Score 0.5 at most if only a level number appears with no move or objective feedback. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Undo or reset is available and visibly restores a previous or initial puzzle state without restarting the executable. Completion and failure states show styled screens with next-level, retry, or title navigation. Score 0 if mistakes force a full restart or completion has no feedback. Score 0.5 at most if only a restart button exists with no undo and no styled result screen. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The laboratory art direction is coherent across grid tiles, magnets, gates, hazards, HUD, title, and result screens, forming a unified visual language. Score 0 if dominated by default controls or unthemed rectangles. Score 0.5 at most when visuals are dominated by primitive shapes, mismatched assets, noisy color choices, or layouts that feel assembled rather than art-directed. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Magnetic interactions have readable animation or effects: field lines, push/pull motion, polarity flashes, gate sliding, sparks, or particle trails that communicate what just happened. Score 0 if all state changes snap silently with no visual feedback. Score 0.5 at most when feedback exists but is minimal, debug-like, or disconnected from the laboratory theme. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.
